text: The surgeon opens the gastrocolic ligament and the epiplomb's electrocavity, preserving the Frédé band to dissociate and release station number six of the gastroepiploic vessels on the right. The dissection is performed by smoothing, with the gastrocolic ligament opened widely. In this corpulent patient, the anatomical planes are more recognizable, aided by the insufflation of gas. Gentle maneuvers are used to avoid cutting the transverse mesocolon.

The surgeon isolates the right gastrointestinal vein with the Henle trunk visible below, preserving it while applying clips for ligation. The gastro-bibloid artery is dissected, revealing a small leakage controlled with a bipolar clamp. An attempt to coagulate the orifice fails, leading to a hemorrhage that obscures the camera view. The surgeon cleans the camera and identifies the blood source to manage the bleeding.

The surgeons locate the bleeding source beneath the clamp and use suction and washing to clear the field, revealing the hole in the right gastrointestinal artery. A clip is applied to achieve hemostasis, but bleeding persists, necessitating a second clip. The clamp is repositioned to identify the highest point, allowing the continuation of the procedure with the section of the gastroepiploic artery near the duodenal junction and further dissection of the duodenum.

The surgeon places gauze for hemostatic control and anatomical landmarks. The iliopathic region is identified, showing signs of lymphangitis, possibly neoplastic or fibrotic. Traction and counter-traction maneuvers reveal the common hepatic artery, which is looped with a colored vessel loop for identification and mobilization. The main biliary duct is identified and released, along with the cystic duct, which is left en bloc. The anatomy is clarified, and a duodenal section is performed to move the operating part from the working field.

The surgeon identifies the right hepatic artery, which courses laterally to the common hepatic duct. Station XIII is isolated and dissected, revealing tenacious adhesions between the biliary tract and the right hepatic artery. Gentle dissection maneuvers separate the cell closely connected to the common hepatic duct. The common hepatic duct and the right and left hepatic ducts are identified, and a vessel loop is used to underpass the main bile duct. The anatomy is highlighted, showing the right hepatic artery behind the common hepatic duct, and the portal branch below. Station XIII emerges as the main road is taken to the left, with dissection maneuvers along the Smussa road.

The surgeon isolates station number thirteen using ultrasonic dissection, focusing on the tip of the station. During the procedure, an aberrant hepatic accessory duct is identified emerging from the common bile duct. A modest leakage is observed and sutured using Prolene 6-0 stitches to reduce it. The aberrant duct is closed at the sinus level in the common bile duct and sent for histological examination. The right branch is highlighted, showing its normal course.

The surgeon performs a regional esophagus anastomosis, isolating the esophagus by seven or eight centimeters under the guidance of a gastrointestinal tube. A linear suture is positioned on the right side of the esophagus with a 45mm charge. The esophagus is partially cut, revealing difficulty as the probe remains trapped, attracting the anastomotic block towards the mediastinum. The probe is palpated and visually identified, and an evaluation is performed in the upper margin. The suture is completed, and the probe is removed, revealing two clips from the mechanical suturing. The probe is repositioned to complete the anastomosis.

The surgeon performs two continuous sutures on the esophagus using a three-zero monofilament, perfecting the right margin with a serous muscle suture to secure the esophagus in the intra-thoracic position. The remaining esophagus is cut, and a second suture line is completed on the left side, consolidating the serous muscle of the suture line. Despite initial concerns about the suture quality, the technique is refined through extensive experience.

However, in order not to create short circuits and in order to perform a better quality of anastomosis, it is preferred to perform a conversion by means of a right subcostal incision and perform a hepatic-digital derivation on ANSA, also excluded from RU, so the final result is that of the highlighted graph, and the patient has two trans-mesocolic diurnal anuses. I think this was a very correct thing from my point of view, to perform a conversion only for esophageal diurnal anastomosis, because only with the help of robotic arms we could perform an anastomosis so small, because this remains an aberrant bile duct or accessory of the right.

At this point the intervention is finished and as it is normally performed with an ERAS also in the upper GI, a radiological control of the esophageal-digital anastomosis is performed in the first post-operative day and the patient begins a feeding in the first hydric day and in the second day free of bones. In the first post-operative day, the presence of about four hundred cc of bile material per day remains in the bile drainage under the bile anastomosis, which is controlled in the following days. This remains for about when we decide to perform a drainage fistulography where we can see that there is a good anastomosis because the medium of contrast passes partly in the biliar duct and we recognize it as a biliar duct accessory on the right also for the size and for the shortness, with the contrast medium that brilliantly goes inside the anus. At this point we see that it is the drainage to maintain this loss, so under radiological guidance the drainage is gradually mobilized, which was probably behaving almost like a care drainage, and at this point the progressive removal leads to a reduction until the total disappearance of the drainage biliary material and the patient is sent home on the twenty-fifth post-operative day in excellent general conditions. This is to say that during a surgical intervention of neoplastic pathology, therefore after a lymphadenatomy, complications may occur, which are not real complications, but rather a problem that can be evidenced during a complex surgical intervention. The important thing is to know how to deal with them and to be able to correct a possible alteration.
